marketName: Data Processing, Hosting, and Related Services

top5Opportunities.statement: Minimize the likelihood of audit evidence rejection due to chain-of-custody gaps between the door guardian physical sensor event and the corresponding SIEM log entry under SOC 2 Type II scrutiny

Minimize the likelihood of an integrity break in chain-of-custody between the proprietary 868 MHz EasyWave radio event and the IP gateway's outbound log payload, producing SIEM entries with no verifiable link to the originating door sensor activation
Minimize the likelihood of door guardian units appearing as online in the gateway dashboard but failing to transmit alarm events under Faraday cage RF conditions, creating undetected monitoring gaps

Increase the ability to generate a SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 A.7.2 compliance report covering all door activation events across a 12-month audit period with per-door, per-event granularity in <=30 minutes

Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit door activation going unlogged in the SIEM audit trail during a facility power event — including UPS switchover, generator start sequence, and mains failure

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of audit evidence rejection due to chain-of-custody gaps between the door guardian physical sensor event and the corresponding SIEM log entry under SOC 2 Type II scrutiny
needs.importanceRationale: SOC 2 Type II audit evidence rejection can block customer trust, delay revenue, and require costly re-audit. Chain-of-custody integrity is a non-negotiable requirement.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired panel (50%) provides native IP audit chain. EasyWave→IP gateway translation (30%) breaks chain-of-custody. Mechanical alarms (20%) produce zero audit evidence. Market-weighted satisfaction is low.
needs.primaryStakeholder: DC Operations Director / Chief Compliance Officer
stakeholderIds: DC Operations Director / Chief Compliance Officer

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of an integrity break in chain-of-custody between the proprietary 868 MHz EasyWave radio event and the IP gateway's outbound log payload, producing SIEM entries with no verifiable link to the originating door sensor activation
needs.importanceRationale: Unverifiable SIEM entries fail SOC 2 CC6.4 evidence requirements; auditors cannot confirm that a log entry corresponds to a real physical door activation event.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No cryptographic audit chain exists between EasyWave radio event and IP gateway payload. The proprietary protocol translation creates an inherent chain-of-custody gap.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of door guardian units appearing as online in the gateway dashboard but failing to transmit alarm events under Faraday cage RF conditions, creating undetected monitoring gaps
needs.importanceRationale: A unit that appears online but cannot transmit alarms creates a false sense of security — the most dangerous failure mode in a DC environment.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No incumbent system distinguishes heartbeat-vs-alarm-test capability. Dashboard online status only confirms radio connectivity, not alarm transmission capability under Faraday cage conditions.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to generate a SOC 2 Type II or ISO 27001 A.7.2 compliance report covering all door activation events across a 12-month audit period with per-door, per-event granularity in <=30 minutes
needs.importanceRationale: SOC 2 and ISO 27001 audits require per-door, per-event granularity over 12 months; manual report assembly takes days, not the 30 minutes auditors expect.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No incumbent provides automated SOC 2/ISO 27001 formatted reports. Wired panels generate raw event logs. Manual aggregation and formatting is the universal practice.
needs.primaryStakeholder: DC Operations Director / Chief Compliance Officer
stakeholderIds: DC Operations Director / Chief Compliance Officer

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit doors being excluded from the SOC 2 CC6.4 monitoring perimeter due to incomplete door inventory at deployment, resulting in >0 unmonitored exits in audit scope
needs.importanceRationale: SOC 2 CC6.4 requires complete perimeter monitoring; a single unmonitored exit creates an audit finding that can fail the entire control objective.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Recalibrated from 5 to 4: wired panel (50%) requires explicit door enrollment in zone programming. In retrofit legacy DCs, secondary exits not pre-wired are routinely absent from panel zone maps.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit door activation going unlogged in the SIEM audit trail during a facility power event — including UPS switchover, generator start sequence, and mains failure
needs.importanceRationale: Power events are the highest-risk periods in a DC; unlogged door activations during these events create the most dangerous audit and security gaps.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired panels (50%) with UPS maintain partial logging during power events. Battery-powered wireless systems (30%) continue operating. Satisfaction is moderate at 6/10 due to UPS coverage.
stakeholderIds: DC Operations Director / Chief Compliance Officer

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of a door guardian unit going offline without automated SOC notification, creating a silent monitoring gap exceeding 15 minutes
needs.importanceRationale: A 15-minute silent monitoring gap in a DC can encompass an entire unauthorized access event without any SOC awareness or audit record.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired panels detect wire breaks. Wireless systems have limited heartbeat monitoring. Automated SOC notification of offline units is not standard across any incumbent technology.
stakeholderIds: DC Operations Director / Chief Compliance Officer

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of receiver placement errors causing >5% of installed door guardian units to report intermittent connectivity loss in a server hall with dense metal rack infrastructure
needs.importanceRationale: Dense metal racks create complex RF environments; >5% intermittent connectivity means multiple doors periodically lose monitoring without operator awareness.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No predictive RF modeling tool exists for server hall rack configurations. Post-deployment signal testing is the only validation method. Receiver placement is trial-and-error.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit door activation — including authorized key override bypass — going unlogged in the SIEM event record, permitting 0 unlogged activations per audit period
needs.importanceRationale: Zero unlogged activations is a hard SOC 2 requirement; even authorized key-override events must appear in the audit trail.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired panels (50%) log panel-level events. Wireless systems log transmitted events but key-override events may not generate a separate log entry. Gap is moderate.
stakeholderIds: DC Operations Director / Chief Compliance Officer

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of incorrect pairing between door guardian radio transmitter and IP gateway causing alarm events to be silently dropped during initial deployment verification
needs.importanceRationale: Incorrect pairing causes events to be silently dropped — the door appears monitored but events never reach the SIEM. This is an undetectable failure.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Learn-mode pairing procedures exist but verification is manual. No automated end-to-end pairing validation exists across 50+ unit deployments.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to verify end-to-end alarm event propagation from each door guardian to the SIEM log within <=30 seconds of a manual test activation across all 50+ installed units simultaneously
needs.importanceRationale: End-to-end propagation verification across 50+ units simultaneously is required for commissioning sign-off; sequential per-door testing takes hours.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No system supports simultaneous 50+ unit alarm propagation verification. Per-door sequential testing is the only method. Market satisfaction is low for large deployments.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to predict door guardian battery replacement needs with >=14 days advance warning per unit, enabling scheduled maintenance without emergency callouts
needs.importanceRationale: 14-day advance warning enables planned maintenance scheduling within DC change management windows, avoiding emergency callouts that disrupt operations.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No wireless exit monitoring system provides 14-day predictive battery alerts. Low-battery threshold alerts provide days, not weeks, of advance warning.
stakeholderIds: DC Operations Director / Chief Compliance Officer

needs.statement: Minimize the time to verify firmware version and radio protocol configuration consistency across all deployed door guardian units via the central BUS gateway without requiring physical access to individual door-mounted hardware
needs.importanceRationale: Firmware version inconsistency across deployed units creates security vulnerabilities and protocol compatibility issues that are invisible without physical inspection.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No remote firmware verification capability exists via the BUS gateway. Physical access to each door-mounted unit is required for version checking.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to deliver door activation alerts to the Security Operations Center console within <=30 seconds of physical door trigger under standard server hall RF conditions
needs.importanceRationale: Sub-30-second alert delivery enables SOC to initiate response while the event is still in progress, increasing intercept probability.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired panels deliver alerts in near-real-time. Wireless systems achieve sub-30-second delivery under normal conditions. Market-weighted satisfaction is moderate.
stakeholderIds: DC Operations Director / Chief Compliance Officer

needs.statement: Minimize the variability in wireless signal quality for door guardian units installed in server halls with variable rack density and AI/GPU thermal load shifts that alter electromagnetic propagation
needs.importanceRationale: AI/GPU thermal loads create dynamic electromagnetic environments; signal quality variability can cause intermittent monitoring gaps that are difficult to diagnose.
needs.satisfactionRationale: No wireless system adapts to dynamic electromagnetic conditions in server halls. Fixed gateway placement cannot account for changing rack density and thermal load patterns.
stakeholderIds: DC Operations Director / Chief Compliance Officer

needs.statement: Increase the ability to update door guardian monitoring configuration — add/remove doors, change alert thresholds — without on-site field work per door, completing a 10-door change in <=1 hour
needs.importanceRationale: DC environments change frequently; remote configuration eliminates the need for physical access to each door within security-restricted zones.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired panels support remote zone configuration. Wireless systems require physical device access for most configuration changes. Market-weighted satisfaction is low.

needs.statement: Minimize the time to restore full door monitoring coverage to 100% of in-scope exits following a gateway or receiver failure, maintaining SOC 2 audit continuity within <=4 hours mean recovery time
needs.importanceRationale: SOC 2 audit continuity requires documented recovery within SLA; extended monitoring gaps create audit evidence discontinuity.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired panels have redundant zone paths. Wireless systems require gateway replacement and re-pairing. 4-hour recovery is achievable for most scenarios.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of RF channel contention from concurrent 868 MHz guard-state broadcasts across a 50+ door deployment in a single server hall causing delayed or silently dropped alarm events during a high-activity security period
needs.importanceRationale: Concurrent broadcasts from 50+ units in a single hall can create channel contention; dropped events during high-activity periods are the worst-case scenario.
needs.satisfactionRationale: EasyWave uses simple time-division without collision detection. No channel hopping or contention management exists for high-density deployments.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to complete RF coverage planning and gateway placement for a 50-door deployment using a standard site survey toolkit in <=1 day of engineer effort
needs.importanceRationale: RF coverage planning for a 50-door DC deployment requires specialist knowledge that adds cost and scheduling dependency.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Standard site survey toolkits exist but are not optimized for server hall environments. Multi-day planning efforts are common for large deployments.

needs.statement: Minimize the variability in SIEM timestamp accuracy between physical door guardian activation and the corresponding log entry to <=5 seconds across all deployed units
needs.importanceRationale: Timestamp accuracy within 5 seconds is required for event correlation with CCTV and PACS records during incident investigation.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Wired panels achieve sub-second timestamp accuracy. Wireless systems introduce variable latency. Market-weighted accuracy is acceptable for most audit requirements.
stakeholderIds: DC Operations Director / Chief Compliance Officer

needs.statement: Minimize the time required to replace IoT module batteries across a 50+ door DC deployment without triggering accidental alarm events during the replacement procedure and without passing any door through an unmonitored state
needs.importanceRationale: Battery replacement in a DC must maintain continuous monitoring and avoid accidental alarms that trigger SOC response procedures.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Key-bypass during replacement suppresses alarms. Monitoring gap during battery swap is brief. The process is manageable with trained technicians.

needs.statement: Minimize the likelihood of alert threshold misconfiguration at deployment resulting in >5% false positive door alarm events per week, degrading Security Operations Center alert response discipline
needs.importanceRationale: False positive rates above 5%/week create SOC alert fatigue, degrading response discipline for genuine events.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Alert threshold configuration is adequate in most systems. Wired panels have established tuning procedures. False positive rates are manageable with proper commissioning.

needs.statement: Increase the ability to install and commission a door guardian unit on any standard commercial exit door within <=15 minutes without specialist low-voltage contractor skills
needs.importanceRationale: Sub-15-minute installation per door without specialist skills enables rapid deployment across 50+ doors with standard DC facilities staff.
needs.satisfactionRationale: Both wireless IoT (30%) and mechanical alarms (20%) achieve sub-15-minute installation. Wired panels require more time but specialist skills are available. Satisfaction is adequate.
